smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC makes premium handmade leather golf headcovers in San Francisco, they run no ads yet, so this call is about starting clean from zero and proving one angle works before we spend real money.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small maker of premium handcrafted leather golf headcovers, hand-stitched in San Francisco. Legal name is Little Prince Customs. The site is a custom Hydrogen build, not standard Shopify, and Leilani is a software engineer who can make site changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Custom and ready-made leather golf headcovers. Three named collections on the site: Quilted (20 designs), Exotic Classic (2 designs, embossed cowhide), and Artisan (8 designs, including rare-material pieces). They offer custom embroidery for initials, logos, or designs. No prices are public, so do not quote any.
deep_dive.positioning: The heritage-craft, premium end of golf accessories. Artisan made and personalized, not mass-produced. Built to last, made for you.
deep_dive.who_buys: Discerning golfers who care about craftsmanship and personal style, and will pay more for a handmade leather cover that stands out on the course and lasts.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: Clear, premium story with a real human maker (Karl) and a made-in-USA hook that is easy to sell in ad creative.
Wide design range in the Quilted line (20 designs) plus custom embroidery, which gives us plenty of creative to test without new product.
gaps: Almost no visible social proof. Only one testimonial on the whole site, so there is little for cold buyers to trust and little for us to put in ads.
No ads running now and no public catalog or prices, so we are building from scratch and cannot yet confirm order value or margin.

game_plan.headline: Start from the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads, prove one angle sells at a small daily budget, then put more behind the winner and add a second product.

phases.goal: Prove that cold Meta traffic will buy at all. Find one ad angle and one product that works.
campaigns: One sales campaign, broad targeting, testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against each other.
A simple retargeting ad for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Lead with a Quilted headcover. It is the collection with the most designs (20), which signals it is their core seller, and the quilted look reads as premium and photographs well in a scroll. We will confirm the actual bestseller on the call before we commit.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet first. Fix the golf bag scale in the image 2 ad using Leilani's real product files before it goes live. Lead with the ad that shows the craftsmanship and made-in-San-Francisco story, because that is the strongest trust angle for a brand with little social proof. Ask for any customer photos or 'compliments on the course' quotes we can add.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can see which ad gets the cheapest clicks and whether any sales come through at this small spend. We are looking for one clear winner, not scale yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and widen the audience.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from Phase 1 with a higher daily budget.
Add 2 to 3 new versions of the winning angle to keep it fresh.
Widen targeting beyond the first audience once the winner is stable.
lead_products: Keep leading with the Phase 1 winner. If Quilted won, test one Artisan design as a premium step-up piece.
phases.creative: Build new ads around whatever message won. Add any real reviews or customer photos we collected. Test the 'build a set, save 10%' offer as an ad angle to lift order value.
phases.budget: Raise spend gradually, only on what is already working.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad holds up at higher spend and sales stay steady as we widen the audience.

phases.goal: Scale the proven winner and add a second product to the mix.
campaigns: Launch a second product line as its own set of ads (for example the Artisan or Exotic Classic collection).
Push custom embroidery as an upsell angle to lift order value.
lead_products: Add the Artisan collection (8 designs, includes rare-material pieces) as a higher-priced second product for buyers who want something more exclusive.
phases.creative: Fresh ads for the second product, using the same voice and craft story. Lean on any social proof built up over the first 60 days.
phases.budget: Scale in steps, backing off anything that slips.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling from ads, a repeatable winning angle, and a stable cost per sale we can keep spending against.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 15 to 25 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which of the 3 ads works before we spend more.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: We have no public prices, no order value, and no margin figures for LPC, so any revenue number would be a guess. Once Leilani gives us average order value and margin on the call, we can set a real target.

assumptions: Quilted is their core seller. If a different collection is actually the bestseller, we lead with that instead.
Their average order value is high enough to support paid ads. If covers are priced low, the plan and budget change.
They can fulfil more orders. Handmade by one artisan (Karl) may cap how fast we can scale.
We can collect at least a few real reviews or customer photos early, since there is almost none on the site today.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta ads for LPC before, even a boosted post?
questions.listening_for: Any past spend, results, or a total blank. If they tried and stopped, find out why. This tells us if we start from zero or from a mess.

questions.question: Who handles marketing right now, and how much time do you have for this?
questions.listening_for: Is it Leilani alone, Karl, or nobody. A one-person brand has limits on both time and how many orders they can fill.

questions.question: What does a typical headcover sell for, and roughly what is left after materials and labour?
questions.listening_for: Order value and margin. Handmade leather with custom work can be pricey, but if margin is thin, paid ads will not pay off. This sets the whole budget.

questions.question: How many covers can Karl make in a week if orders spiked?
questions.listening_for: The real ceiling on scale. If he can make ten a week, we cannot promise big volume in 90 days.

questions.question: Which collection or design sells the most today, Quilted, Artisan, or Exotic Classic?
questions.listening_for: Confirms or corrects our plan to lead with Quilted. Whatever already sells is what we put in front of cold buyers first.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site, how many actually buy versus just browse?
questions.listening_for: A rough sense of whether the site converts. If people click but never buy, the problem is the site or price, not the ads.

questions.question: Do you have any customer reviews, photos, or messages we could use, even ones not on the site?
questions.listening_for: Anything real to build trust in ads. This is the biggest gap. Even a handful of texts or DMs would help a lot.

questions.question: How long does a custom order take to make and ship?
questions.listening_for: Long lead times can hurt ad performance and returns. Custom items are also non-returnable, which we need to be clear about in ads.

questions.question: Do you ever see people finding you by searching for things like custom golf headcovers or leather headcovers?
questions.listening_for: Golfers do search for headcovers by name, so there is a real opening for Google search ads later. If they say people already find them that way, that is a green light to add search once Meta is working.

questions.question: Are you showing up on Google today when someone searches your product, or only your brand name?
questions.listening_for: If they only rank for their own name, there is room to capture people searching the product. Worth an SEO or search conversation in phase 3, not now.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and do not want to burn money.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start at a small daily budget with the 3 ads I already built for you. Month one is only about learning which one works. We do not spend real money until something proves it can sell.

objections.objection: Everything is handmade by one person, so we cannot handle a flood of orders.
objections.answer: Good. We scale in steps, not overnight. We match spend to what Karl can make. The plan does not promise a flood in month one, it promises one proven winner first.

objections.objection: Our site is a custom Hydrogen build, not normal Shopify.
objections.answer: No problem at all. Having you as a software engineer on the brand side actually makes changes faster. The ads run on Meta, not on your site, so the build does not slow us down.

objections.objection: We barely have any reviews to point to.
objections.answer: True, and that is why the first ads lead with your craft and made-in-San-Francisco story instead of star counts. Part of the early plan is helping you collect real reviews and photos so later ads get stronger.

red_flags.flag: Almost no social proof on the site. Just one testimonial.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold buyers of a premium product need trust. With little proof, early ads have to work harder, and collecting reviews should be part of month one.

red_flags.flag: No prices or catalog are public, so we do not know order value or margin.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot promise revenue or set a real budget until we know these. Kyle must ask, not guess.
This caps how fast we can scale. Promising big volume fast would set us up to fail.

red_flags.flag: Custom items are non-returnable and buyer pays return shipping.
red_flags.why_it_matters: This can cause complaints if not made clear in the ad and on the product page. Worth flagging so we set expectations up front.

other_channel_openings: Golfers do search for terms like custom golf headcovers and leather headcovers, so Google search ads are a genuine option once Meta is proven, likely in phase 3.
If they only rank on Google for their brand name today, there is room for SEO and search to capture people already looking for the product.
